passages.explanation: They cross the lake to the region of the Gerasenes. When Jesus gets out of the boat, a man with an impure spirit comes from the tombs to meet him. He lives among the tombs and no one can bind him any more — not even with a chain. He's been chained hand and foot, but he tears the chains apart and breaks the irons on his feet. No one is strong enough to subdue him. Night and day he cries out among the tombs and in the hills, cutting himself with stones. When he sees Jesus from a distance, he runs and falls on his knees before him. He shouts: what do you want with me, Jesus, Son of the Most High God? In God's name don't torture me! Jesus asks: what is your name? My name is Legion, for we are many. He begs Jesus again and again not to send them out of the area. A large herd of pigs is feeding on the nearby hillside. The demons beg Jesus to send them into the pigs. He gives them permission. The impure spirits come out and go into the pigs — about two thousand — and the herd rushes down the steep bank into the lake and drowns. Those tending the pigs run off and report this. People come and see the man who had been possessed by the legion of demons, sitting there dressed and in his right mind — and they're afraid. Those who saw it tell them about the man and the pigs. Then the people plead with Jesus to leave their region. As Jesus gets into the boat, the man who'd been demon-possessed begs to go with him. Jesus doesn't let him. Instead: go home to your own people and tell them how much the Lord has done for you. The man goes and tells everyone in the Decapolis how much Jesus has done for him, and everyone is amazed. Mark gives this story the most detail of any miracle — the man's condition is horrifying and vivid. The townspeople prefer the predictable horror of a demoniac to the unpredictable power of Jesus. They choose pigs over people. And the man who's freed becomes a missionary to a Gentile region Jesus won't visit again.

Jesus crosses back over and a large crowd gathers by the lake. Jairus, a synagogue leader, falls at Jesus' feet and pleads earnestly: my little daughter is dying. Please come and put your hands on her so she'll be healed and live. Jesus goes with him and a large crowd follows, pressing around him. A woman has been subject to bleeding for twelve years. She's suffered under many doctors, spent everything she has, and instead of getting better, she's grown worse. When she hears about Jesus, she comes up behind him in the crowd and touches his cloak — she thinks: if I just touch his clothes, I'll be healed. Immediately her bleeding stops and she feels in her body that she's freed from her suffering. Jesus immediately knows that power has gone out from him. He turns around in the crowd: who touched my clothes? His disciples say: you see the people crowding against you, and yet you ask 'who touched me?' But he keeps looking around to see who has done it. The woman, knowing what's happened to her, comes and falls at his feet, trembling with fear, and tells him the whole truth. He says: daughter, your faith has healed you. Go in peace and be freed from your suffering. Mark sandwiches this healing inside Jairus' story — a literary technique he uses throughout the Gospel. While a synagogue leader's daughter is dying, Jesus stops for an unnamed, unclean woman. She's been bleeding for twelve years; the girl is twelve years old. Both are called 'daughter.' Jesus treats the invisible woman with the same urgency as the prominent man's child.

While Jesus is still speaking, people from Jairus' house arrive: your daughter is dead. Why bother the teacher any more? Overhearing this, Jesus tells Jairus: don't be afraid — just believe. He allows only Peter, James, and John to follow him. At the house he sees a commotion — people crying and wailing loudly. He goes in and says: why all this commotion and wailing? The child isn't dead but asleep. They laugh at him. He puts them all out, takes the child's father and mother and the disciples with him, and goes in where the child is. He takes her by the hand and says: Talitha koum — which means 'little girl, I say to you, get up.' Immediately the girl stands up and begins to walk around. They're completely astonished. He gives strict orders not to let anyone know about this and tells them to give her something to eat. Mark preserves the Aramaic words — you can hear Jesus' actual voice. 'Little girl, get up' is tender, not theatrical. And the practical instruction to feed her grounds the miracle in reality: she's not a ghost, she's a hungry child who needs lunch.
